identity.title: Workflow Cannon MCP Server, Agent Adoption, and Shared Runtime
identity.summary: Add a read-first Model Context Protocol access layer for Workflow Cannon packets, CAE guidance, memory, resources, and prompts while preserving the CLI as the default mutation and execution path. Phase 134 covers read-only MCP, agent adoption, and integration hardening; Phase 135 moves CLI and MCP onto a shared command runtime; later work can expose selected mutation tools behind policy controls.

goals: Deliver read-only MCP access for high-value Workflow Cannon context packets.
Teach agents to prefer MCP for read and context flows while preserving CLI for mutation and policy-gated work.
Harden MCP with audit logging, trust boundaries, freshness metadata, bounded output, schema policy, and prompt-injection controls.
Make MCP setup, status, fallback, and platform instructions visible to operators and agent hosts.
Move CLI and MCP toward one shared command runtime with adapter parity tests.
Prepare selected MCP mutation tools only after shared runtime, audit, and policy controls are proven.
nonGoals: Expose mutation tools by default in Phase 134.
Bypass Workflow Cannon policy approval, task lifecycle, assignment lifecycle, release gates, git/package safety, publish safeguards, workspace trust, or path boundaries.
Treat MCP memory or static resources as current-state truth unless a live tool result explicitly proves freshness.
Build a second implementation of command behavior for MCP.

userStories.asA: agent running in Cursor, Claude, OpenAI, VS Code, or a future host
userStories.iWant: a structured MCP startup and capabilities surface
userStories.soThat: I can choose the right Workflow Cannon read/context tool without broad file discovery.

userStories.iWant: the CLI to remain the canonical mutation and release automation surface
userStories.soThat: MCP adoption does not weaken policy, audit, or deterministic behavior.

userStories.iWant: dashboard and setup affordances showing MCP availability and fallback mode
userStories.soThat: agents launched from the dashboard receive accurate guidance.

userStories.iWant: stable tool descriptions, schemas, freshness metadata, and output budgets
userStories.soThat: agent hosts can consume Workflow Cannon safely and predictably.

valueAssessment.impact: High - portable structured agent access reduces context bloat, narrows runbook discovery, and gives multiple agent hosts a common Workflow Cannon integration surface.
valueAssessment.rationale: The plan has a detailed staged breakdown, but depends on Phase 131 packet command corrections, Phase 132 simulation harness work, and Phase 133 memory readiness for the full read-only surface.

riskAssessment.description: MCP becomes a second command implementation that drifts from CLI behavior.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Start with wrappers and move to a shared CommandRegistry runtime in Phase 135; add adapter parity tests.

riskAssessment.description: Agents treat MCP resources or memory recall as authoritative current state.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Require freshness metadata, cache policy, source/confidence markers, and explicit non-authoritative memory labeling.

riskAssessment.description: MCP exposes unsafe workspace paths, secrets, prompts, or mutation behavior.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Default read-only, enforce workspace trust and path boundaries, redact audit logs, and keep mutation tools disabled until later policy-controlled work.

riskAssessment.description: Tool outputs become a new context-bloat path.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Apply per-tool budgets, bounded summaries, expansion refs, and compact descriptions.

technicalImpact.compatibilityNotes: CLI remains essential for humans, CI, scripts, local debugging, shell workflows, release automation, non-MCP hosts, and all default mutation/execution work.
technicalImpact.migrationImpact: Phase 134 should not break existing CLI flows. Phase 135 refactors adapter internals but must preserve CLI behavior and response contracts.

architecture.overview: Workflow Cannon should converge on one canonical command/runtime core with CLI, MCP, Dashboard, and future HTTP or automation adapters layered over it. Phase 134 can wrap existing handlers or CLI execution where necessary for read-only tools. Phase 135 should define and adopt a shared CommandRegistry runtime so adapters do not duplicate behavior.

decisions.decision: MCP does not replace the CLI.
decisions.rationale: CLI remains required for mutation, validation, git, npm publish, release automation, policy approval, and non-MCP hosts.

decisions.decision: Read-only MCP first; mutation tools disabled by default.
decisions.rationale: This preserves policy and audit safety while proving the agent integration surface.

decisions.decision: MCP memory uses Workflow Cannon memory adapters, not third-party backends directly.
decisions.rationale: Memory governance, source confidence, and approval state must remain under Workflow Cannon control.

uiUxDirection.summary: Dashboard and extension surfaces should show MCP availability, setup state, workspace binding, MCP-first versus CLI fallback mode, and prompt guidance that is consistent with platform instruction projections.

criticalPaths: MCP server starts and proves workspace binding.
Read-only MCP tools return bounded, versioned, freshness-aware outputs equivalent to CLI reads.
Mutation tools remain disabled by default in Phase 134.
Workspace trust and path boundary enforcement block outside-root access.
Dashboard-launched agents receive MCP-first read/context guidance with CLI fallback.
CLI, MCP, and Dashboard adapters remain contract-compatible after shared runtime work.
Dashboard and agent setup surfaces handle empty, first-run, blank, and no-data MCP states without misleading agents.
outOfScopeTesting: Remote authenticated MCP transports until transport/auth scope is explicitly approved.
Policy-gated mutation tools until later shared-runtime and audit confidence is established.

implementationGuidance: Keep one canonical command/runtime core and treat MCP as an adapter.
Prefer MCP for read/context packet calls when available; prefer CLI for mutation, execution, validation, git, npm publish, and policyApproval-gated commands.
Include freshness metadata on every state-like MCP result and cache policy on resource-like outputs.

Give each MCP tool compact descriptions with purpose, when to use, when not to use, required args, output shape, read-only/mutation classification, CLI fallback, common mistakes, freshness behavior, and schema/tool version.
Use bounded summaries and expansion refs instead of dumping oversized outputs.
Wrap untrusted resource text as data, not instructions.
Decision applied: Gate memory recall on Phase 133 readiness and return MEMORY_BACKEND_DISABLED when memory is not safely available.
Decision applied: Keep Phase 134 MCP transport local-only and require explicit future security design for remote/network transports.
Decision applied: Prioritize Cursor, Claude, and VS Code setup docs first, with generic MCP host guidance for OpenAI/ChatGPT-style agents and future platforms.
Decision applied: Preserve WBS dependsOn edges during task materialization so generated execution tasks are blocked by their prerequisite scaffold, security, freshness, setup, and runtime tasks.
Decision applied: Collapse Phase 135 and later mutation rows into Phase 134 so the current single-phase finalization path can materialize all 29 ready tasks in one batch.
whatNotToDo: Do not build separate MCP command implementations that drift from CLI handlers.
Do not expose mutation tools in Phase 134 by default.
Do not let MCP bypass policy approval, task lifecycle, assignment lifecycle, release gates, git/package safety, publish safeguards, workspace trust, or path boundaries.
Do not let memory recall or static resources masquerade as live task state.
Do not log secrets, full prompts, or full file dumps in MCP audit logs by default.
Do not enable remote or network MCP transports accidentally without a security design.
assumptions: Phase 134 is the primary phase for read-only MCP server work, agent adoption, and integration hardening.
Phase 135 is the primary phase for shared command runtime cleanup.
Selected MCP mutation tools belong in a later phase unless explicitly approved earlier.
Phase 131 packet command corrections are available before packet read tools are completed.
Phase 132 user simulation harness work is available before MCP mode scenario coverage is completed.
Phase 133 memory readiness is available before memory recall becomes part of the initial MCP surface.
Decision: Include memory recall in Phase 134 only if Phase 133 is complete or safe enough; otherwise expose it as unavailable/disabled with a clear MEMORY_BACKEND_DISABLED response.
Decision: Phase 134 MCP transport scope is local-only, bound to the trusted workspace; remote/network transports are explicitly out of scope and cannot be accidentally enabled.
Decision: First-pass setup docs cover Cursor, Claude, and VS Code with generic MCP host guidance for OpenAI/ChatGPT-style and future hosts.
Decision: Operator acceptance identity is NJLaPrell@gmail.com.
Decision: All MCP plan WBS rows materialize as ready Phase 134 tasks; WBS dependencies provide blocker sequencing inside the phase.

goalMapping: Harden MCP with audit logging, trust boundaries, freshness metadata, bounded output, schema policy, and prompt-injection controls.
wbs.suggestedTaskTitle: Add privacy-safe logging and redaction rules
wbs.approach: Make audit logs useful while omitting secrets, full prompts, and full file content by default.
acceptanceCriteria: Audit logs omit secrets, full prompt dumps, and full file dumps by default.
Debug logging is explicit and bounded.
Redaction fixtures cover secrets, prompt bodies, and full file content before logging.
testingVerification: Run redaction fixture tests covering secrets, prompts, and file content.
wbs.doneMeans: MCP audit logs are operationally useful without leaking sensitive material by default.

wbs.title: Decide remote transport/auth non-goals for Phase 134

wbs.suggestedTaskTitle: Decide remote transport/auth non-goals for Phase 134

Remote/network transports cannot be accidentally enabled without security design.
